rationale: A live credential must never be persisted. Record WHERE it comes from (env var, vault path), never the value.
PEM/SSH private keys are the crown jewels; persisting one is an incident in itself.
user:pass@host connection strings leak a working credential in one line.
Long-lived session tokens are replayable; a token in memory outlives the run that found it.
Directly identifying and legally sensitive; there is no review reason to retain it.
PCI scope. Never store a PAN, even partially, in review artefacts.
Real user rows pulled from a database belong in the database, not in a report. Describe the shape, not the row.
A person's residence. The finding is 'an address is exposed here', which needs no address to be understood.
Personal contact detail; the location of the leak is the finding, not the digits.
Personal contact detail. A documented public support address is PASS - use judgment.
Combined with a name it is strongly identifying and useful for account recovery attacks.
Names of non-public individuals (users, patients, customers). Public maintainers in git history are PASS.
Lat/long or GPS traces locate a person; the class of exposure is the finding.
Special-category data under GDPR/HIPAA; never copy the values into an artefact.
Needed to correlate the same host across runs, but publishing internal topology aids an attacker. Hash so it stays comparable without being readable.
A published support address or company phone is already public; redacting it destroys useful context.
Public by definition and load-bearing for a finding's reproducibility.
file:line is the whole point of a finding. Repo-relative paths are not PII.
